title: Gaza is not a blank space
dek: Any future built over Palestinian voices is not peace. Gaza’s people are not a problem to be managed; they are a people with rights, memory and political agency.
imageAlt: Palestinians inspect the ruins of Aklouk Tower in Gaza City in October 2023
imageCredit: Naaman Omar / APAimages via Wikimedia Commons
quote: To stand with Gaza begins with a simple refusal: Palestinians cannot be erased from the story of their own land.
paragraphs: Gaza is too often described only through the vocabulary of crisis: aid deliveries, security zones, casualty counts and reconstruction plans. Those facts matter, but a people cannot be reduced to the systems that fail them. Gaza is also family, work, sea, school, humor, faith, memory and an insistence on a future.

A rights-based view starts with Palestinian civilians as human beings, not as scenery around a military conflict. Their safety is not conditional. Their homes are not abstractions. Their ability to remain on their land, participate in political decisions and live without forced displacement must sit at the center of any serious proposal.

Nearly all of Gaza’s population had been repeatedly displaced by late 2025, according to the UN humanitarian appeal. The scale is so vast that displacement has become a shared social condition, not an exceptional event. Yet even a statistic this large cannot show what it means to lose a street, a neighbor, a family archive or the place where a child expected to grow up.

Standing on Gaza’s side does not require denying anyone else’s humanity. It requires rejecting the idea that Palestinian humanity is negotiable. Protection for civilians, accountability under international law, unrestricted humanitarian relief and meaningful Palestinian self-determination are not radical demands. They are the minimum vocabulary of justice.

The future of Gaza must be written with Gazans, not for them. Any plan that imagines the land while excluding its people repeats the logic of dispossession. A durable peace will need more than silence from weapons; it will need freedom, rights and a political horizon Palestinians can call their own.

chart.title: A population repeatedly displaced
chart.description: By 31 October 2025, the UN reported that nearly all 2.1 million people in Gaza had been repeatedly displaced.

title: A generation deserves its classrooms back
dek: Education is not a luxury to postpone until after survival. For Gaza’s children, learning is part of survival—and part of recovering a future.
imageAlt: The damaged UNRWA Zaitoun Boys School in Gaza
imageCredit: Abedallah Alhaj / UNRWA via Wikimedia Commons
quote: Every destroyed classroom is also an attack on time—the years children cannot recover by simply waiting.
paragraphs: A classroom gives a child more than lessons. It gives rhythm to the week, trusted adults beyond the family, friendships, play and evidence that tomorrow can be planned. In Gaza, the destruction of education has taken all of these at once.

UNICEF reported at the end of 2025 that 95.5 percent of Gaza’s 815 schools had been damaged or destroyed. All 637,000 school-aged children lacked access to formal education. These numbers describe a system-wide collapse, not a temporary interruption.

Children do not pause their development during war. Lost school years arrive alongside bereavement, injury, hunger, repeated displacement and fear. Temporary learning spaces can offer connection and safety, but they cannot substitute indefinitely for a functioning education system with trained teachers, materials and stable buildings.

Rebuilding schools must therefore begin early and be led with Palestinian educators, families and students. It should include accessible design, mental-health support, restored digital infrastructure and protection from future attack. Education must not be treated as a decorative final stage of reconstruction.

To be on Gaza’s side is to defend its children’s right to imagine professions, relationships and lives beyond emergency. The world should not ask them only how they survived. It should help restore the conditions in which they can choose who they become.

chart.title: Gaza’s damaged school system
chart.description: UNICEF reported that 95.5% of all 815 schools were damaged or destroyed by the end of 2025.

sourceName: UNICEF State of Palestine report, December 2025

title: Food and medicine are rights, not bargaining chips
dek: Humanitarian aid should reach civilians according to need. It must never be treated as political leverage, a reward or a substitute for rights.
imageAlt: Destruction in Deir el-Balah, Gaza, in August 2024
imageCredit: Ashraf Amra / UNRWA via Wikimedia Commons
quote: Aid can keep a person alive today. Rights are what allow that person to build tomorrow.
paragraphs: The language of aid can make the basics of life sound like generosity. Food, clean water, medicine, shelter and sanitation are not gifts granted to a population for good behavior. They are protected needs, and civilians must be able to access them safely and consistently.

Humanitarian agencies have worked under extraordinary risk in Gaza, but trucks and temporary distributions cannot repair destroyed water systems, hospitals, roads and homes. OCHA reported in October 2025 that 78 percent of Gaza’s estimated 250,000 buildings had been damaged or destroyed, generating around 61 million tonnes of debris.

When infrastructure collapses, every delivery becomes harder. A bag of flour cannot replace a functioning market. Bottled water cannot replace a water network. A field clinic cannot carry the long-term load of a health system. Emergency relief is essential, but it cannot be used to normalize permanent deprivation.

Humanitarian access must be predictable, large enough for the need, and designed around civilian safety. It also requires protecting aid workers, medical personnel, journalists and local civil servants whose work keeps communities functioning.

Solidarity with Gaza means insisting on both immediate access and the political conditions that make endless relief unnecessary. People deserve not only to receive supplies, but to produce, trade, travel, heal and organize their lives with freedom and dignity.

chart.title: Damage across Gaza’s built environment
chart.description: OCHA cited a UN environmental assessment estimating that 78% of Gaza’s 250,000 buildings were damaged or destroyed by October 2025.

title: Rebuilding Gaza must belong to Palestinians
dek: Reconstruction is not merely a construction project. It is a question of who decides, who returns, who owns, and whether Palestinians retain control over their future.
imageAlt: Aerial view of destruction in Beach refugee camp, Gaza
imageCredit: Abedallah Alhaj / UNRWA via Wikimedia Commons
quote: Reconstruction without Palestinian agency risks rebuilding walls while reproducing the conditions of loss.
paragraphs: The word reconstruction can sound technical: budgets, debris, concrete, timelines. But every choice about what is rebuilt, where it is rebuilt and who controls it is political. A home is not interchangeable with any housing unit, and a neighborhood is not simply empty land available for redesign.

The 2026 Gaza Rapid Damage and Needs Assessment estimated recovery and reconstruction needs at about $71.4 billion. It divided the work into immediate, short-term and medium-to-long-term phases, with housing representing the largest sectoral need.

Money alone cannot guarantee a just recovery. Palestinians must lead planning at every level—from national institutions and municipalities to engineers, teachers, health workers, business owners and neighborhood committees. Property claims must be protected, displacement must not be made permanent, and rebuilding contracts should strengthen Palestinian capacity rather than create dependency.

Recovery also means restoring systems: electricity, water, hospitals, universities, transport, farms, workshops and digital networks. It means paying workers, reopening local businesses and allowing materials and expertise to move. The measure of success cannot be the number of structures completed; it must be whether people can live with security and control.

Gaza should not become an experiment designed around everyone’s interests except those of Gazans. The principle is straightforward: reconstruction must preserve Palestinian presence, ownership and self-determination.

chart.title: Phases of Gaza’s estimated recovery need
chart.description: The 2026 World Bank-led assessment estimated $71.4 billion in total recovery and reconstruction needs.

sourceName: Gaza Rapid Damage and Needs Assessment 2026

title: Gaza’s memory is part of its future
dek: Gaza should not be remembered only through ruins. Its coast, craft, architecture, archives and stories are part of a Palestinian cultural life that deserves protection.
imageAlt: Palestinian fishermen at Gaza seaport in 2018
quote: To protect Gaza’s culture is to protect evidence that Palestinian life has depth, continuity and a future.
paragraphs: Before Gaza became a permanent headline, it was—and remains—a Mediterranean society with fishermen, poets, farmers, architects, artisans, teachers and families whose histories extend far beyond the current war. Remembering this is not nostalgia. It is resistance to the flattening of a place into devastation alone.

Cultural heritage carries identity across generations. A historic house records how people built and lived. A mosque, church or monastery holds layers of community memory. A museum collection, family photograph or handwritten archive can connect someone to a city even after displacement.

As of March 24, 2026, UNESCO had verified damage to 164 cultural sites in Gaza: historic and artistic buildings, religious sites, monuments, archaeological locations, museums and repositories. The count is an assessment of verified physical damage, not a complete measure of cultural loss.

Protection and restoration should be led by Palestinian historians, conservators, artists and communities. Digital documentation matters, but preservation cannot end with scans stored elsewhere. Heritage must remain connected to people, place and living practice.

Being on Gaza’s side also means refusing to let destruction define the final image. The sea is still part of Gaza. So are its recipes, dialect, music, embroidery, books and memories. A just future makes space for all of them to continue.

chart.title: Gaza cultural sites with verified damage
chart.description: UNESCO had verified damage to 164 cultural sites as of 24 March 2026.

sourceName: UNESCO assessment of cultural heritage damage
